title: Chapter Thirteen: The Offer
content: The painting stops me in the doorway.

It hangs alone on the far wall of Rowan's office — a Renaissance portrait, oil on panel, a man in green silk with a lace collar and painter's hands resting on the arm of a carved chair. His cheekbones could cut glass. His cheekbones could cut me. They're Rowan's cheekbones, transplanted four hundred years into the past, and the resemblance is so precise it feels like a trap.
"Fourteenth century," Rowan says from behind the desk. "Northern Italian. The attribution is disputed."
"I can see why," I say, and I look away from the painting because looking at it feels like looking at the answer to a question I haven't asked yet.

The office is a study in controlled minimalism. Glass walls on two sides, looking out over the Foundation's atrium below and the Manhattan skyline beyond. A desk so clean it might never have been used. A single chair for visitors — one, not two, as if he only ever expects one person at a time. The air smells like fresh paint and possibility, like a room that's been renovated but never moved into, and I wonder if that's the office or Rowan himself.
"Sit," he says. Not a command. An offering.
I sit. The chair is leather, soft enough to swallow me. I perch on the edge instead.

Rowan slides a folder across the desk. Not a thick one — maybe ten pages — but the paper is heavy, cream-laid, the kind that costs more per sheet than my rent-per-square-foot. I can feel the texture through my fingertips, the slight tooth of expensive stationery, and something about the weight of it makes my hands tremble.
I open it.
*The Ashford Foundation is pleased to offer Ms. Orlando Reyes the position of Designer-in-Residence, with the following terms:*
Studio space at the Foundation's atelier. Dedicated, private, north-facing light.
Funding. A monthly stipend that — I read the number twice, three times — would cover my rent, my materials, and still leave enough to quit at least two of my three jobs.

Creative autonomy. The words sit on the page like a key turning in a lock I didn't know I'd been pushing against. *Creative autonomy.* Freedom to design what I want, how I want, for the Foundation's exhibitions and collections. No creative direction imposed. No aesthetic mandates. Just — make.
Something unlocks in my chest. A door I've been leaning against for years, and behind it is a room I've only seen in sketches, in dreams, in the shape my hands make when I'm not thinking.
Then I read the next word.
*Exclusive.*
The door slams. The lock turns the other way.

*"Designer-in-Residence shall provide services exclusively to the Ashford Foundation for the duration of the contract, refraining from commercial or collaborative work with any outside entity without prior written consent—"*
I close the folder.
"Design for me," Rowan says. His voice is quiet, unhurried. "For the Foundation. For whatever you want."

The silence stretches. I can hear the atrium below — footsteps on marble, the distant clink of a cart, someone laughing at something I can't see. The glass walls make everything visible and nothing private. I think about the Brooklyn studio with its surging radiator and the neighbor who plays saxophone at two in the morning and the water damage spreading across the ceiling like a map of a country I'm tired of living in. I think about the three jobs, the pinched fingers and the aching back and the way I fall into bed too tired to dream.
I think about exclusive.
"No," I say.
Rowan doesn't blink. He waits.
"Maybe," I say, and I hate the way my voice sounds — thin, uncertain, nothing like the woman who corrected an auctioneer in a catering uniform.
I try again. "I need to think about it."

"Take all the time you need." He leans back in his chair, and his composure is absolute — the relaxed shoulders, the level gaze, the mouth that gives nothing away. But his right hand is turning the emerald ring on his index finger, a slow rotation, thumb against stone, and that tell I'm learning to read tells me more than his voice ever could. He's not as calm as he's pretending to be.
"Why me?" I ask.
He stops turning the ring. Just for a moment. Then he starts again, and his mouth does something I haven't seen before — a half-smile, crooked, unguarded, gone almost before it arrives.

"Because you're the only person I've ever met who looks at a garment like it's a living thing." He pauses. Lets the words settle. "And because you corrected an auctioneer in a catering uniform and you were right. A Worth evening cape, 1889, misidentified as a walking jacket." Another half-smile, half-ghost. "That takes either genius or insanity."
He looks at me.
"I'm betting on genius."

The lobby of the Ashford Foundation is all marble and light, the kind of space that makes you conscious of the sound your shoes make. I'm halfway to the elevator when I pass her — Maren Voss, steel-haired, jaw like a ship's prow, standing near the reception desk with a tablet in her hands and an expression that suggests she's cataloguing everything in the room, including me.
Her eyes track me as I walk past. Not a glance — a scan. Top to bottom, one pass, like she's reading a garment's construction from the outside in. A nod. Nothing more.
I nod back.

The elevator closes, and I'm alone with the hum of the cables and the faint scent of whatever cleaning product they use on these glass panels — something sharp and institutional, nothing like paint and possibility, nothing like the smell of a room that wants you in it.
I press the button for the ground floor and I don't look back.

Suki is organizing fabric bolts when I get home. She's arranged them by color — warm to cool, left to right — which is not how I organize them, and I would mind except she's also unfolded three yards of charcoal wool that I'd been saving for a collared cloak, and she's draped it over the dress form with pins already marking the shoulder seams, and it looks better than anything I've managed in weeks.
"The offer," I say, dropping my bag on the floor. "From the Foundation."
Suki looks up from the pink silk she's holding. Her eyes widen. "Tell me."
I tell her. Studio space. Funding. Creative autonomy. Exclusive.
"Orlando." She says my name like it's a announcement. "That's the biggest break you've had since I've known you."
"I know."
"But?"

I sit on the floor because the chair is covered in muslin and the radiator is making that sound again — the clunking, the surge, the hiss of something pressurized that doesn't know when to stop. I pull my knees to my chest.
"Closeness triggers things I can't explain."

Suki gives me a long look. The gum stops chewing. I've known her for three years and I've seen her stop chewing gum exactly four times — once when her landlord raised her rent, once when her mother called, once when I showed up at her door at midnight with blood on my sleeve from a sewing machine accident, and now.

"Babe." She puts down the silk. Crosses to me. Sits on the floor across from me with her legs folded beneath her like she's been waiting for this conversation since the day we met. "You've been surviving for as long as I've known you. Don't you want to find out what living looks like?"

I don't answer. The radiator clunks. Somewhere in the building, someone is cooking — garlic and sesame oil, the smell drifting through the gaps in the walls, and it occurs to me that I haven't eaten since morning, that there's cold leftover takeout in the fridge from two nights ago, and that the taste of reheated lo mein at midnight is the taste of every night I've spent in this room, surviving.
Suki reaches over and squeezes my hand. Once. Hard. Then she picks up the silk and goes back to work, because Suki knows that some things can't be talked through — they have to be decided in silence.
After she leaves, I unlock the drawer.

The sketchbook is navy blue, soft-cover, the kind I bought at a art supply store on Canal Street four years ago. The pages are warped from humidity and pressure — from my hands pressing too hard, from being shoved into bags and under pillows, from the weight of everything I've poured into them and couldn't say out loud.
I open it.

The same silhouette. Page after page after page. Seven centuries in my blood, or that's how it feels — the design I was born to make. It's a gown and it's an armor and it's a confession, and every time I sketch it, I get closer, and every time I sketch it, I know I'm not close enough. The bodice is structured but fluid, boning that follows the body's architecture instead of forcing it into shape. The skirt moves like water but holds like stone. The neckline — I've redrawn the neckline forty times, and I still don't have it right, because the neckline is where the garment meets the throat, and the throat is where the voice lives, and the voice is the thing I've been swallowing for as long as I can remember.

I need space to make this. Real space. North light. Ceiling height enough to hang a full-length muslin without cramping. Fabric — yards and yards of it, the good stuff, the silk and the wool and the linen that drapes like it's been waiting for hands like mine. I need the Ashford archive, with its four centuries of textile history, its rare weaves, its documented provenance. I need resources I will never, ever have in a Brooklyn studio with a surging radiator and three jobs and a bank account that flinches every time I buy thread.
I close the sketchbook.
I need to stop being afraid of what proximity does to my body.

That's the real sentence. The one I couldn't say to Suki. The one I can barely think in the privacy of my own skull. Because it's not just the studio or the funding or the archive — it's him. It's the way the air changes when he's in the room. It's the way my skin hums at a frequency I can't name, a low vibration that starts at the base of my spine and radiates outward until my fingertips tingle and my chest tightens and I'm not Orlando anymore, I'm something else, something fluid and electric and absolutely terrifying.
Sable's voice in my head: *Closeness triggers the shift.*

I know. I know it does. I've felt it happen — the blurring at the edges, the way my sense of self softens and spreads when he's near, the way I become more and less than myself simultaneously. It's not attraction. It's not desire. It's something older than both, something that lives in the spaces between cells, something that has been moving through bodies for seven hundred years and chose this body, this moment, this impossible choice.
The studio or the fear.
The resource or the distance.
The treasure or the cage.
I stare at the sketchbook. The silhouette stares back.
At eleven PM, I call Rowan.
The phone rings once.

"Orlando." He says my name like he's been holding it in his mouth for hours, waiting for the chance to release it. One ring. He was waiting. The ring turns in the dark — I can't see it, but I know it, I can feel the emerald rotating beneath his thumb, the way I can feel a garment's grain direction without touching it.
"I'll do it," I say. "But I have conditions."
"Name them."

I take a breath. The room is dark except for the streetlight through the window, orange and gritty, casting a rectangle of polluted light across the floor. The cold lo mein sits on the counter. I can taste it on my tongue — stale, metallic, the flavor of every compromise I've ever made. Not anymore.
"No visits to my studio without calling first."
"Agreed."
"No touching the garments while I'm working."
"Agreed."

I pause. The last one is the hardest. The last one is the boundary I'm building between myself and the hum, between myself and the shift, between myself and whatever happens when proximity dissolves the distance I've been maintaining like a wall made of air.
"And if I tell you I need space — you give it. No questions."

Silence. A long one. Long enough that I can hear his breath, slow and steady, and the faint creak of leather — he's sitting in that chair, I realize, the one behind the glass desk, in that office with the painting and the ghost of green silk and the cheekbones that match a dead man's portrait. He's there, and I'm here, and the distance between us is thirty blocks and a phone line and every wall I've ever built.
"Done."
The word lands soft and certain, like a stitch in exactly the right place.
A pause. Then:
"Orlando?"
"Yes?"
"Welcome to the Foundation."

I hang up the phone. The screen goes dark. The room is quiet except for the radiator, the street, the hum of the city that never stops. I sit in the dark and I feel it — the shift already starting, the edges of myself beginning to blur, the way water reaches for land not because it wants to but because that's what water does.
I've moved into his world. I've set the rules that will keep me safe.
But the hum doesn't respect boundaries.
And neither does love.
